video_title: Mastering SAT Readiness with Khan Academy SAT Prep

segments.text: Hello everyone and thank you so much for joining us. We have the Khan Academy team here to
discuss live with Denver Public Schools on preparing every student for success. So if you haven't
connected with us in a while, welcome from Khan Academy. We are a nonprofit with a mission of the
bottom line of learning and not profit. We've been in the business for nearly 20 years now and a
reliable choice in ed tech and then we're designed for teachers and students powered by responsible
AI grounded in learning science. So I wanted to give a quick introduction to the fabulous team. We'll
have joining us today from the Khan Academy side. We have Jennifer who heads up our professional
learning with Denver Public Schools and Cody who leads the district success and implementation there.
And then we also have the fabulous Dr. Ted Thompson Denver Public Schools executive director of
secondary schools. And who's going to share with us kind of the secret sauce about what's been
working in Denver in preparing students with the SAT. And before I kick it over to them,
just wanted to give a quick note that we are the official SAT prep partner. And in partnering with
College Board to co create the SAT courses, this means that our mastery based SAT courses on
Khan Academy are built with real SAT content and are fully aligned to the skills teachers are
already teaching in their classrooms. So this ensures that materials with the SAT's content,
rigor, format are all being matched. And this is a free resources that students could use to build
these skills, gain confidence and improve their test scores. And so you'll hear a bit about what
our free offering is and as well as ways we can partner deeper. And just a quick glimpse so you
can kind of see what that would look like. Essentially students would take those practice tests on the
College Board site. I believe to the Blue Book app. And then it will directly link them to practice
here with us. So when they review that practice test, it would say view that score. And then they
have scored details of the ability to practice on Khan Academy. And so without further ado, I'm
going to pass it over to Cody and Jennifer and kind of walk through the implementation details and
what that looks like in practice. Perfect. Thank you so much, Chelsea. Good morning, everyone.
We're just going to kick it right off. Dr. Thompson, I would love for you to set this scene for us.
What was the challenge that Denver Public Schools was trying to solve and why did SAT preparation
become a district level priority? Yeah, sure. Again, thanks for having me. When we started in August,
we had about 33 secondary schools. It's about 19,000 students that all had a different approach
of preparing students for co-set testing in the spring. And so given that we wanted to better
understand school approaches to planning and which plans impacted achievement outcomes for
students the most, the reason why it's a district priority is because of this impact on the state
performance framework. So there are three indicators on the state performance framework, achievement,
growth, and PWR. And all those are primarily measured by SAT impacting grades 9, 10, and 11.
And for DPS, that's about 14,000 students. Wow. So our platform,
tell us, Chelsea pointed out we are the official college board partner, right? A lot of districts
have made that SAT prep available. But what has made Denver Public Schools decide that access alone
wasn't enough? What did you actually change? I mean, I think for us, you know, access did not
necessarily translate into consistent use by schools. And so because we have a variety of partners
offering prep options and, you know, we have a variety of secondary courses in literacy and math,
we really needed something that was specific and scheduled to help us understand skills growth,
that was needed for SAT success. And we wanted to make sure that those skills were being explicitly
supported during instructional time. So I think, and again, like this was a very new endeavor.
We had this last year, a new team came in over the summer, and we're really trying to make sense
and make meaning of current plans that were in place. But again, given all that, we decided to be
more strategic around the data we use to inform prep tearing of students and establishing a monitoring
schedule to better understand skills growth weekly for crop for our schools.
So when you take on something to this magnitude, there's a lot of alignment that has to happen. So
who would you say had to be aligned internally for this work? And where did you hit if any friction?
I think we had universal alignment around the purpose of this work.
Again, because schools had varying experiences based on the partner, I think what we wanted to make
sure that there was clarity around was messaging and design for monitoring the work cross schools
based on the partnership between schools, academics, and the accountability team.
I would say the friction came in with the support from various partners to provide data
and professional development. And quite honestly, given the capacity of partners, I think we move
to working with Khan more intentionally because you all have support to really like
embrace the infrastructure that was conducive for us to support large-scale implementation.
Thank you so much. Thank you for mentioning our partnerships. So I'm going to pop over to Cody
for just a second. Cody, as the district success manager, really honed in on implementation
from your vantage point of working with districts across various states, what separates the ones
that make real progress from the ones that struggle? Well, I think that the example that Dr. Thompson
has set with his site leaders in looking at moving skills to proficient and mastery
really helps move student learning. One request he had was how do I see what skills are growing
in their mastery level? And we really wanted to look at that change happening. So going beyond time
and looking at the increase in skills, Denver really was able to show a tremendous increase
in how many skills students were working on. And all of our efficacy research shows that moving
skills to proficient or mastered is what makes the change in learning outcomes. So that was a big
impact for Denver this year so far. And that sounds like that's on the road to accountability
and visibility, which is a huge part of implementation. So Dr. Thompson back to you thinking about
what Cody has just highlighted in terms of looking at skills to proficient, making those comparisons.
When we think about accountability and visibility, how do your district leadership teams stay
informed on progress? And then what does that oversight for you and your team actually look like
from day to day? Yeah, I received by weekly reports from Khan and it shares school-level data
and district trends. I review that data with my network directors who are in charge of supervising
various clusters of schools. And then we identify areas of focus based on the skills gap report
that shared by Khan. The schools teams will then share those reports with their IOT instructional
leadership teams that are monitoring each week to ensure that students are hitting the 30-minute
benchmark for the varying sessions that we have dedicated to Khan. For the most part, I feel like
our relationship has evolved over the months because we've partnered intimately with our
academics team who has basically used our curriculum scope and sequence to support identifying modules
based on Khan lessons to kind of create a blended experience for students so they're getting a
regular touch point when SAT style questions and other skills areas throughout their two-one
instructional experience. I love that you have mentioned the the the isolated SAT practice but also
bringing in those folks from curriculum and having students have access to that practice while
they're learning that content as well. I think that's a powerful statement that you just made.
Cody, Dr. Thompson told us exactly what he wanted to see what his what his leadership team wanted to see.
How did you help structure that from the start of the partnership?
Well, I think just really giving the team at Denver and Dr. Thompson specifically what we know
works with Khan Academy and then site level data I think really helped him drive it with the
schools and then him actually giving us access to work with site leaders directly really helped us
move each individual site in the right direction and giving them the knowledge that they needed to
to really see what was happening with the SAT prep and specifically practice practice courses.
So I think it's important that district leaders know who the impact makers are on campus and who are
the ones that can and can make sure that it's actually happening on campus and also giving him that
data I think really helped him have the tools to go and talk with his site leaders and network leaders.
So it's important to know who is implementing on campus and who is responsible for that and that made
a big difference. And Dr. Thompson, you mentioned earlier it wasn't just about access. It was being very
intentional putting it in front of students, giving them the opportunity to prepare and as you've
pointed out, doing that in a variety of ways. The isolated practice as well as infusing it into
tier one and first instruction. Equity is often a reason that districts take on something also like
Khan Academy and district wide scaling. Do you think that this model has delivered at this point
in the year? Do you feel like this model has delivered what you were looking for and for which
students do you see this being most meaningful to change their trajectory for the test?
I think it has. I think however, like when we launched at the beginning of the year, our primary
goal was figuring out how do we scale? So how do we one make it accessible? We'll make sure the
right student groups are being exposed to the prep platform to ensure that we had enough measures
to start to really pack data to better understand performance across the segregated groups.
And so I think as we look at the data currently, it's very promising. We continue to monitor skills
growth at the proficient and mastery levels. We continue to compare multiple practice at bats
toward the fall PSAT, SAT administration in October. And we're seeing some very promising results
for groups that historically have not excelled in their prep experience. So time will tell a few weeks
and it's the big test. So we're excited about what we're thinking about and we're also planning for
the future with the Khan team, but also trying to be mindful of our current assessment schedule
because I think for us, the more practice at bats, we can give students the more comfortable they are
and preparing for the test. And I think we've tried to balance that with all the other administrative
assessments that we have to provide, but hopefully next year we can come up with a sequence that is
balanced and appropriate for building confidence for students so that they go into the test positive.
I know that I'm definitely excited about your student outcomes. And I've enjoyed working with
all of your campuses and the leadership team and the teachers alongside Cody. This next question
is for both of you guys, Cody and Dr. Thompson. What would you tell a superintendent who might be
attending today or watching this on demand who is interested but is not sure that they have the
capacity right now to take this on? Dr. Thompson will start with you and then Cody will move to you
from a district implementation standpoint. Yeah, I mean, I think I would offer
there's limited capacity across many systems, especially in Colorado with a lot of the
budget challenges at the state level. But what I would say is it's really not about your
internal capacity, it's really the capacity of the partner. And I think this year,
consibility to adapt and support given our school realities and our limited like planning window,
we had a week or two to get this thing off the ground. Cons efforts have been hurtly in and I'm
very appreciative of the partnership and the collaboration. We meet weekly in some cases by weekly,
like Cody is always available, even on vacation, which I try not to take that time from her.
But I've just really appreciated the flexibility and adaptability. And I think that's the key to this partnership.
Perfect Cody, what about you? What would you say to a superintendent who was interested in con,
a con partnership and SAT preparation, but not sure that they have the capacity from your standpoint?
I think the good news is we like Dr. Thompson pointed out we are adaptable.
So we can we can kind of help fit in wherever you're at and whatever your immediate goals are.
But you do have to, I mean, I used to be a former site leader. If something else is kind of put on my
shoulders, I have to have support to make that happen. And I need to understand my clear objective
and my goals and what's available to me. So you got to kind of think through that a little bit
before you sign up for something, make sure that the site level folks
are set on a path that helps with success because it's hard to get another thing piled on top of you.
You know SAT is important, you know you want your kids to do well.
Having a clear idea of how they can do that is important. And we're thankful that we were able to
work with different site leaders and they all had all different contexts. And we could kind of talk
through what their best approach would be. And that was pretty powerful. In some cases, we've
worked with districts where it's just a one size fits all approach. And Dr. Thompson's been really
good about like really letting us work with those sites and make sure that they know the objective,
but we kind of help them think through what the appropriate implementation would be and push them
through the nuances of their own schools. So I think it all can be done and we're willing to help
with with whatever context is there for that district. Thank you, Cody. We've heard a lot in just
a short period of time today about the implementation for SAT preparation in partnership with Khan Academy.
And I just have one final question. It's really about next steps and it's for both of you, Dr.
Thompson and Cody. If a district or superintendent is in the room today or is watching on demand and
wanted to start having a real conversation about this, what would you say would be the right first step?
Dr. Thompson will start with you. Sure. Yeah. I would say a session of current infrastructure for
system wide implementation for prep and then contact Khan. Basically, I think for us,
what do you have? Right? And then know your vision for what you want to see at the end of this
experience as far as outcomes for students. And that's enough to get you started.
Perfect. Thank you. Cody, what about you? What would be the right first step?
I think that that was very well said, Dr. Thompson. And then just give us a call and we'll have an
inquiry conversation on what your structure is and what your goals are and how we can best support
and the learners in your school district. Perfect. Well, thank you so much, Dr. Thompson and Cody
for your sharing today. Just for your your mind and your energy. Thank you, Dr. Thompson,
for your continued partnership in Denver Public Schools. I do want to remind everyone that
Denver Public did this at scale. And you heard straight from Dr. Thompson what that looks like.
It's all about the skills to proficient and making sure that students are getting that time
and intentionality to prepare for SAT. I do want to kick it back over to Chelsea. She's got
a few things to wrap us up. So Chelsea, back to you. Thank you. I love that I get to feel like a
news anchor. I appreciate it Jennifer. Thank you all so much for sharing. And just for absolute
kind of clarity and reinforcement, I think you'll you'll see here that again, we have a free offering.
We have free tools you could go in today and start using our SAT prep courses in preparation for
the upcoming assessment. Or you can discuss deeper what a partnership could look like. And I think
the big difference between success and stagnation isn't just the software. It isn't just the
tool at hand. It's the system around it. And so these people on this call having intentionality
and working together to make that implementation work. It was the human system around Denver's
implementation that has made it so successful. Not just, you know, some magic pill of economy course.
So I thank you. Thank you so much for all your guys's dedicated work. But a couple of things to
note that if you are in district partnership with us, formal district partnership, that some of
what comes with it, right, is that that human piece, the supports and implementation systems,
and as well as the courses and content that are research proven. And then along with AI for
students. So some of you may have heard of Conmigo. But what we do see is informal partnership that
districts are eight to 14 times more likely to reach recommended levels of learning compared to
independent use of Khan Academy. And what that recommended learning looks like is backed based on
that mastery learning system on those skills to proficient. And so what Cody was touching on
earlier earlier that Dr. Thompson and all of the team has been tracking. And so really what we're
aiming to see is like two ish skills to proficient a week. And hoping that around 30 to 60 minutes a
week of practice and then learning on Khan Academy. And then again, another component that is a
part of that district offering is Conmigo. That is the AI component of personal digital SAT tutor for
students. And it provides just in time support and instant assistance and feedback helps engaging
curiosity and asking questions. But it's another resource that layers on top of that quality content.
And then for any of you that are, you know, exploring SAT partnership and are already working with
College Board again as the official partner of College Board. We do have an even deepening
partnership in terms of contracting. So if you're already purchasing the full suite of SAT assessments
through College Board, you can now add on Khan Academy as a district partner at a reduced rate of
$10 per student. And so if that's something you're interested exploring or would like to learn more
about how to deepen a partnership with Khan Academy, please feel free to reach out. And you can
scan this QR code here. And that will bring you to a demo form. You can select SAT and it will
reroute yeah. To me, as the rep for the West and for Colorado. And another thing to note is at the end
of this, if you've registered, you will get a replay of the event to watch on demand. We do have an
upcoming Superintendent's Fireside Chat webinar with Amy Azina and Sal Khan this Friday, March 27th at 11 a.m.
Mountain time. And if you have any direct questions, you're happy. I'm happy to answer those. You can
always email me directly at Chelsea Hatcherd at Khan Academy dot work. But again, thank you so much
for your time, Dr. Thompson and Jennifer and Cody for leading such a fabulous partnership. We really
appreciate you. And we hope to connect soon.
